Hello and thanks for the patch.

editdistance is a problematic dependency because it is not pure Python. This causes issues with alternate implementations of Python such as PyPy or Jython. In addition, that project has no installable wheels on PyPI, which means the library has to be built when installing. The user may not have a compiler or the right dependencies installed, especially on Windows, which would cause the install to fail. I can't accept that dependency, sorry.

Could difflib be used instead? See https://docs.python.org/3/library/difflib.html#difflib.SequenceMatcher.ratio  
It does pretty much the same as editdistance, except with a different result format.

Other things:

* Please follow PEP8. Some of your lines are over 80 characters.
* For the feature itself, I would suggest moving it to a method of CliSignature instead.
* Consider using the builtin min or max functions instead of a for loop in this case.
* There isn't always going to be a "good" match. You should figure out a threshold for discarding irrelevant guesses. For instance if the only aliases in the signature are ``-h`` and ``--help``, they are still not relevant suggestion if you typed ``--verbose``.
* The final patch will need tests, and I think test-driven development would help with figuring out the right threshold. If you need help figuring out the tests structure let me know. I know they could stand some explaining.
* For backwards-compatibility, errors.UnknownOption(name) should still work. In fact, it could perhaps handle calling the "find the best match" method, since it has access to the CliBoundArguments instance. This also has the effect of leaving the parser loop untouched.

I know my bug description was vague, and so is the guidance for contributing. Sorry about that. I'll work to fix that.

Is there any reason to not use `relativedelta` from the `dateutil` package (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/python-dateutil)? It provides a very robust way to get "next month" and is a well-known module:

import datetime

# In [1]: datetime.date.today()
# Out[1]: datetime.date(2015, 12, 1)

# In [2]: next_month
# Out[2]: datetime.date(2016, 1, 1)

> Il giorno 09 nov 2015, alle ore 13:27, Kyle Barbary <notifications@github.com> ha scritto:
> 
> There's a reason, but it's not a great one, and I am thinking about changing it. Here is the story:
> 
> FITS files are stored in big-endian byte order. All modern CPUs are little-endian byte order, so at some point you have to byteswap the data. Some FITS readers (cfitsio, the fitsio python module) do the byteswap when reading the data from disk to memory, so you get numpy arrays in native (little-endian) byte order. On the other hand, astropy.io.fits does not byteswap for you, and you get numpy arrays in non-native byte order. However, most of the time you never notice this because when you do any numpy operations on such arrays, numpy uses an intermediate buffer to byteswap the array behind the scenes and returns the result as a native byte order array.
> 
> Within SEP, we're not using numpy operations, we're just getting a pointer to the data in the array and passing it to C code, so we can't take advantage of the fancy buffering byteswapping numpy does. We could write our own buffer code, but that would be work. A simpler option would be to check if the array is non-native byte order, and if so, make a byteswapped copy of the entire array. Easier to code, but higher memory use. In addition, this would have to be done repeatedly in multiple SEP functions: Background, extract, sum_circle, etc. Each would make a copy of the entire data array.
> 
> I wanted SEP to be extremely high-performance like SExtractor itself, including minimal memory usage. Repeatedly making a copy of the entire data array without the user knowing seemed like a bad idea - it seemed better to explicitly tell the user to do the byteswap operation themselves so they could just do it once.
> 
> The ideal solution in my mind would be for astropy.io.fits to return native byte order arrays. I doubt that change will happen though, so maybe SEP should allow non-native byte order arrays somehow.
> 
> —

@whiteplanet 
Thank you for the help! I find the error only take place randomly when using hiredis as parser.

The `CancelledError` is catched by `HiredisParser.read_response` and raised as a `ConnectionError`, which will be retried in error handling by reconnection in `pubsub.parse_response`. Reconnection will invoke `HiredisParser.on_connect`, till now every thing runs well, then connection try to use parser to parse response, code is like below:
```Python
async def read_response(self):
        if not self._stream:
            raise ConnectionError("Socket closed on remote end")

        # _next_response might be cached from a can_read() call
        if self._next_response is not False:
            response = self._next_response
            self._next_response = False
            return response

        response = self._reader.gets()
        while response is False:
            try:
                buffer = await self._stream.read(self._read_size)
            except Exception:
                e = sys.exc_info()[1]
                raise ConnectionError("Error {} while reading from stream: {}".format(type(e), e.args,))
            if not buffer:
                raise ConnectionError("Socket closed on remote end")
            self._reader.feed(buffer)
            response = self._reader.gets()
        if isinstance(response, ResponseError):
            response = self.parse_error(response.args[0])
        return response
```
The problem is, `self._reader` is not None before loop(if it is then `self._reader.gets()` will raise Exception) but after awaken by event loop from `await`, `self._reader` is set to None, and `self._reader.feed(buffer)` raise error. Since connection and parser are in one to one relationship and there is only one connection in use by a pubsub instance, it seems threadsafe. 
I think it may be caused by the unsubscribe after breaking the loop(caused by CancelledError), there may be event not awaken and handled when first CancelledError raised. And when it coming to be handled, connection is disconnected and parser._reader is set to None.(That's why nothing happened without unsubscribed)

All above is my speculation, i am not so sure. But to prove my guess i add `asyncio.sleep` before unsubscribe, no error raised then. What's your opinion?

Thanks for the informative input and link, Martin.

Yes, I also initially went searching for a corresponding `**set**lastmodified` or just `lastmodified` in the RFC and code, but without any luck. In fact the commands both result in `PROPPATCH **get**lastmodified` requests.
I uploaded a trace of `touch` from the original at:
http://www.erda.dk/wsgidav-touch-trace.txt
and one from the version with my added support for changing it at:
http://www.erda.dk/wsgidav-touch-enabled-trace.txt

So apparently one *must* handle getlastmodified for it to work, but it could of course easily be extended to cover plain lastmodified requests as well.

Doing another `touch` after the first one resulted in first a HEAD request that received a matching
`Last-Modified: 'Tue, 14 Jul 2015 06:15:02 GMT'` reply, and then the actual next PROPPATCH request. I don't know if that would confuse other clients, but in our case I'd say it's the intended result.
I guess the same clients would then also be confused if a user would mimic a `touch` by moving the original file out of the way and then moving another copy with just a different time stamp there, because then that different time stamp is transferred.

I'm going to be mostly away for the next week or two, but will take a look at making a configurable implementation when time permits. I'm thinking something along the lines of adding support for a 
mutableliveprops = ["{DAV:}getlastmodified"]
conf option and forwarding any matching live PROPPATCH requests in dav_provider.setPropertyValue if the actual provider has a corresponding setX method. 
Comments and suggestions are welcome...

The config.h file is install to the same include folder that will be used whether you are compiling for a debug dlib lib or a release dlib lib. If you leave out DLIB_ENABLE_ASSERTS during the cmake build you will end up with dlibd.lib (debug lib, built with ENABLE_ASSERT) and a dlib.lib (release lib, build without ENABLE_ASSERT) and a config.h

```
/* #undef ENABLE_ASSERTS */
#define DLIB_DISABLE_ASSERTS // asserts always disabled 
```

so when you compile your app in debug using dlibd.lib it will fail. If you compile in release with dlib.lib it will succeed. If define DLIB_ENABLE_ASSERTS  for cmake it will work for both.

> Edit: obj.asdict() seems to be more correct. It appears the new dataclass in py3.7 will have a asdict method

Regarding `dataclasses`: As far as I can tell, `asdict` is not a method of dataclasses but rather a helper function:

```
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict

@dataclass
class C:
     l: List[Point]

p = Point(10, 20)
assert asdict(p) == {'x': 10, 'y': 20}
```

An option wold be to use [`tinydb-serialization`](https://github.com/msiemens/tinydb-serialization). Other than that, there's no real standard for serializing arbitrary python objects to dictionaries. I also agree with @therden that making something like `doc.as_dict()` a magic method that - if present - adds serialization would go against the ideal of explicitness.

Hello,

I strongly recommend against trying to compile CLIPS and CLIPSPy yourself as it is a quite tedious procedure. I just uploaded both 32 and 64 bit statically linked version on PyPI.

Therefore, you should be able to install them yourself via: `pip install clipspy`.

In case you want to persevere in your masochistic path here how you can do it (statically linked library).

Retrieve the CLIPS sources from [this link](https://sourceforge.net/projects/clipsrules/files/CLIPS/6.30/clips_core_source_630.zip) and build them with a recent enough version of Visual Studio. Make sure the static object is named `clips.lib`

From CLIPSPy root folder build using this command:

```
python setup.py build_ext --include-dirs=<path-to-static-object-folder> --library-dirs=<path-to-CLIPS-headers>
```

To generate the `wheel` file:
```
python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel
```

You can then install the wheel as usual:
```
pip install <path-to-the-wheel>
```

The main reason why this issue hard to resolve is because Fabric's `remote_tunnel` opens local port on the target host. When Docker daemon tries to pull image for example from 'localhost:5000' it adds wrong `Host` header to the request. This causes 503 HTTP error as described [here](https://docs.docker.com/registry/deploying/#important-required-http-headers).

The only possible way to solve this issue is to add alias 'docker.io' to `/etc/hosts` which points to 'localhost' (127.0.0.1).

Also there is a way to [make alias only for current user](https://github.com/thypon/hostaliases). This module can be useful if installed on the target host(s).

I experienced the same recursion issue when working with the root logger. As soon as you set a log level and attach a CloudWatchLogHandler to the root logger, boto (namely botocore as seen in your traceback) starts logging its own own messages as well. Those in turn are forwarded to the CloudWatchLogHandler which creates a neverending loop. One solution is to attach a filter to the CloudWatchLogHandler like this one:

```
def boto_filter(record):
     return not record.name.startswith('botocore')

handler = CloudWatchLogHandler()
handler.addFilter(boto_filter)
```

So, in PowerPoint 2010, when a chart is selected in the slide, in the *Insert* menu it is possible to add a text box.  This text box will be put in a file called `drawings/drawing1.xml`, and this file will be referenced in `charts/_rels/chart1.xml.rels`:

```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="yes"?>
<Relationships xmlns="http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/package/2006/relationships">
  ...
  <Relationship Id="rId4" Type="http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/officeDocument/2006/relationships/chartUserShapes" Target="../drawings/drawing1.xml"/>
</Relationships>
```

The file `drawings/drawing1.xml` looks like this:

```
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<c:userShapes xmlns:c="http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/drawingml/2006/chart">
  <cdr:relSizeAnchor xmlns:cdr="http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/drawingml/2006/chartDrawing">
    <cdr:from>
      <cdr:x>0.06145</cdr:x>
      <cdr:y>0.06258</cdr:y>
    </cdr:from>
    <cdr:to>
      <cdr:x>0.31487</cdr:x>
      <cdr:y>0.23488</cdr:y>
    </cdr:to>
    <cdr:sp macro="" textlink="">
      ...
    </cdr:sp>
  </cdr:relSizeAnchor>
</c:userShapes>
```

AFAICT the `python-pptx` library has no support for those so-called *chart user shapes*.  I will rename the title to better reflect this.

@thomaspurchas first let me briefly describe the threads in HAP-python and their purpose:
- The server thread - this listens for iOS clients and spawns a new thread to handle each new connection.
- The accessory threads - This is either one thread or multiple threads, depending on if there is a single accessory or a bridge containing multiple accessories. Each of these runs the `Accessory.run`. Eventually, value updates (such as reading the temperature from a sensor) result in an event being sent to iOS clients. This is achieved by each thread writing the event to a thread-safe queue (the event queue).
- The "send events" thread - Because we don't want the Accessory threads to block waiting for the event to be sent, there is another thread which reads from the event queue and sends updates to clients. This also handles stale connections, etc.

The biggest "thread overhead" comes from the accessory threads because you have N threads for N accessories. What is more, they mostly sleep and do GPIO (e.g. read the sensor every X minutes). So if I have to outline a roadmap for this:

- [x] Move from accessory threads to a mix of coroutines and threads.
- [ ] Evaluate if moving to [aiohttp](https://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) is worth it. This is not clear, because each client retains an open two-way connection and I am not entirely sure that moving to asyncio will be better. Some factors are the average number of clients and the average frequency of events coming from the accessories.

Let me step back a few points.

The problem I see with the current setup is that we create the event loop within the driver (which is the right call IMO). As we move more and more methods to async. Eventually nearly all objects will need a reference to the `loop` to work.

To be honest I took this lesson from Home Assistant as well. Their we have the `hass` object as a starting point (and the event loop handler) and basically any object created has some kind of reference to it.

----

> separate things

Their is nothing against that. The question that remains however is how much sense does it make to have a object that is totally separated, but can't do anything since it requires some setup (eg. `set_driver` and `set_sentinel` for the accessory)?

> Tests

You're right their. However currently you need an accessory to test the top level driver. HA has a special function to handle this `get_test_home_assistant()`. It returns a `hass` object to use for tests.

> Move state from accessory to driver

I still plan on doing this.

---

The way I see it the hierarchy for `HAP-python` looks / could look something like this:

```text
-> AccessoryDriver
  -> AccessoryState (or a different name)
  -> Loader (should be cached here instead of the module)
  -> Encoder
  -> HAPServer
    -> ...
  -> Accessory
    -> IIDManager
    -> Service
      -> Characteristic
```

- `__delitem__` should raise KeyError or some other exception. Not return a boolean;
- Most method use `self._h._resource_serialize(...)` to serialize their input before sending it to Heroku. But the `__setitem__()` method doesn't and uses the `json.dumps()` function directly;
- `to_dict()` exposes the internal state of object: it `return self.data` meaning a code like this:

```python
config_vars = app.config()
d = config_vars.to_dict()  # or === config_vars.dict()
# From here on d and self.data reference the *same* mutable data structure !!
del d['SOME_SETTING']
if 'SOME_SETTING' not in config_vars:
    print('Oh My !')
```
Would print `'Oh My !'`. I am not sure this is a good idea. One should copy the dictionary referenced by `self.data` before passing it on to the user. Otherwise, the state of the object is inconsistent with the actual state of the application as stored in Heroku infrastructure.

Hi Jeff. Thanks for you work and interest. 
Overall, the changes on your branch looks good. Yes, I think making context as the last parameter is easier to deal with. I'm thinking even making it optional for both actions and recognizers as most of the time it's not needed. Something like:

```python
try:
   # call action or recognizer without context
except TypeError:
   # call it with context
``` 
This might introduce problems if `TypeError` is raised by the actual user code though.

I think your change in `_token_recognition` is actually a fix as recognition should be considered unsuccessful *only* if `None` is returned.

Please keep in mind that parglare can parse not only strings but any Python sequence (e.g. list of objects), Please see [here](https://github.com/igordejanovic/parglare/blob/master/tests/func/test_recognizers.py). So token recognizers may return recognized sub-lists. Also, in that case, layout is a list too.

Cloning context in GLR might be a good idea. I was going the route of reusing the same object due to performance reasons but not sure that there is much of a gain achieved given the number of assignments needed to reconstruct the state of the context at each iteration.

That has to due if the files are of a person speaking in a hall like a
college class setting. In there you have alot of high peaks of the
speaker's voice. Then when they stop speaking the dB floor drops past -32
dB when that happens any leveling tool will not help much because most
leveling tools use a over all bass estimate of high and low dB in the whole
file to try to get it over all as close to the volume that you want in dB.
But if you have spots where due to echo's or poorly setup sound system and
then the speaker stop speaking for a few seconds then you base low dB noise
floor it below -32 dB when that happens most leveler's will crop the lower
noise floor dB to -32 dB. Because most people have trouble hearing audio
pass -32 dB without amplification. That way you will need to run a low pass
filter before you level the file that will help. Try to keep the low pass
set at -30 to -26 dB. That will rase the lower noise floor by removeing the
lower blank dB part's so they now are higher dB in those parts. Then most
leveling software should be able to now do it thing. By the way you may
need to raise you sampling rate that you files are done in and reduce the
bitrate of the audio file from 320 kilobytes per second to say 96 kilobytes
per second. That will save more file room and keeping more of the audio as
it was recorded. If you reduce the sampling rate from 44.1K or 44100 to say
36K or 36000. You are not compassing the audio what are doing it remove a
lot of the audio info. So that is part of way you have those high swings
because once that information is lost no software can make it better. I
hope this helps. If not fill free to email back to me and I'll be glad to
help you out more.

We've used redis lock to avoid duplicated login entries in our MySQL database since unique index was not available in our specific use case. We've used controlled execution, something like this:

```python
with my_lock:
    do_login()
```

Now imagine the following situation:

Client A -> Acquires lock and for any given reason gets suck with the lock, **renewing** it before it expires.
Client B -> Tries to acquire lock **forever**.

We need to return execution after X seconds and return something to the user. Of course we could have used Lock.acquire(), but what does 'with' statements stand for?

And as much as I know, it's not possible to set a retry count for Lock.acquire() or Lock.__enter__().

`win_unicode_console.enable()` has to be done first, since it doesn't wrap anything. It creates new streams and the original ones are replaced. Colorama wraps any present stream and filters out and interprets the ANSI control sequences.

The problem here is early binding behavior of `progress`, which stores the stderr stream at import time, so it doesn't consider the stream object might change. First calling `win_unicode_console.enable()`, then `colorama.init()`, and then importing `progress` should work.

I've been looking into this too. The way Flask-Security currently does its own HMAC handling is ... odd ... and I think non-standard in terms of passlib's expectations. Flask-Security currently calculates a secure hash twice: first by calculating the HMAC of the password and second by calling CryptContext.encrypt, which does it again. Additionally, it's using a constant salt for the first step, rather than relying on passlib to provide an automatic salt (which they recommend).

The pending patch in 72db52426f396be55540e7140b181d9a40b32ca7 as part of #324 makes a lot more sense to me because it delegates to CryptContext directly using the *password*, not the HMAC of the password. However, I suspect it's not backwards compatible since upon upgrade, old passwords wouldn't match correctly.

Any comments from anyone on the state of #324?

I did some research. You have forgotten to mention that you are using `ManifestStaticFilesStorage` for your static files. Indeed, in this case `staticfiles_storage.url()` won't work with `DEBUG = False` because you have a "Catch 22" situation when `collectstatic` tries to collect a file that is not there yet. The solution is to temporarily switch to `DEBUG = True`, collect your static files and then switch back.

Also while your PR may fix a problem with collecting statics in your setup, it breaks the application completely because you won't have a correct URL for TinyMC4 main file `tinymce.min.js` in your rendered HTML pages. This may be the reason for your issue #14.

I'm closing this PR as invalid. Next time please do more research and testing yourself before submitting a PR.

This example demonstrates the intent that `reorder-python-imports` has:

```python
from __future__ import absolute_import

import sys
from sys import version_info

import pre_commit
from pre_commit import main

import mymod
from . import bar
from .submod import baz
from mymod import foo
```

[From this documentation](https://github.com/asottile/reorder_python_imports#separates-imports-into-three-sections)

Internal imports *are* always after third party modules (including explicit relative imports).  Explicit relative imports are sorted lexicographically with other internal imports

Very well said by Julien Simon. A couple of additional points to expand a bit on point 4:

1. SageMaker also helps to decouple model hosting and inference from the rest of the ML pipeline. If you just used Spark ML, you'd generally need to load your data into a `DataFrame` to make predictions by `transform`ing your `DataFrame` after loading your model and your data in your cluster. While SageMaker Spark lets you transform a `DataFrame` using a SageMaker-hosted model, you don't _need_ to use SageMaker Spark to make inferences after your model is trained and hosted -- you can get predictions using the [InvokeEndpoint API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/API_runtime_InvokeEndpoint.html), as SageMaker Spark does internally.

2.  Latency for small-batch predictions. It can be difficult to achieve low-latency predictions with Spark ML models (though some projects like [MLeap](https://github.com/combust/mleap) make this achievable). Spark is really well-suited for inferences on large `DataFrame`s, but due to various overhead costs Spark incurs, not for small batches of data. You can get real-time predictions with models hosted in SageMaker.

```python
np.einsum('i...,i->...', randn(3,3,3), randn(3))
```

but adding this test to tests/test_systematic.py makes it fail inside `unbroadcast_einsum`:

```python

the error is "ValueError: output has more dimensions than subscripts given in einstein sum, but no '...' ellipsis provided to broadcast the extra dimensions." and it's because `new_subscripts` is `...,i...->i'`[when `einsum_unbroadcast` calls `einsum`](https://github.com/yukoba/autograd/blob/7e8bddcf7db3048ecb6e69a7ed8badba6e3811f7/autograd/numpy/numpy_grads.py#L443).

The tech provided two kinds of timeout, one is per-state timeout and another is total angr instance timeout.
the start time maybe not so accurate so a better way maybe to do as @rhelmot suggests, about the state timeout, current implementation perhaps is not good, you perhaps should consider using simgr's step callback to do per-state time-counting and timeout checks if this functionality is needed at all...

I'm new to angr too, so please keep doubting every line of my codes...

`
import angr
from angr import ExplorationTechnique
import sched, time

import logging
l = logging.getLogger('angr.rlimiters')

PRUNED_SLOTNAME = "pruned"

class TimeLimiter(ExplorationTechnique):
  """
  limit simulation manager's total wall time or state's runtime in seconds
  """
  def __init__(self, max_time=24*3600, max_state_time=None):
    super(TimeLimiter, self).__init__()
    self.max_time = max_time
    self.max_state_time = max_state_time
    self.scheduler = sched.scheduler()

    self.all_timeout = False
    self.timeout_states = set()

  def _on_time_consumed(self):
    self.all_timeout = True
  
  def _on_engine_process_pre(self, state):
    state.globals['__tl_pre_time'] = time.time()

  def _on_engine__process_post(self, state):
    ctag = time.time()
    ss = state.inspect.sim_successors

    for s in ss.flat_successors:
      stag = s.globals.get('__tl_pre_time', ctag)
      cstime = ctag - stag
      if cstime > 0:
        kt = '__tl_stime'
        if kt not in s.globals:
          s.globals[kt] = 0
        s.globals[kt] += cstime
        if s.globals[kt] > self.max_state_time:
          self.timeout_states.add(s)

  def setup(self, simgr):
    if self.max_time:
      self.scheduler.enter(self.max_time, 1, self._on_time_consumed)
      self.scheduler.run()

    if self.max_state_time:
      state.inspect.b('engine_process', when=angr.BP_BEFORE, action=self._on_engine_process_pre)
      state.inspect.b('engine_process', when=angr.BP_AFTER, action=self._on_engine__process_post)

  def filter(self, state):
    if self.all_timeout:
      return PRUNED_SLOTNAME

    if state in self.timeout_states:
      self.timeout_states.remove(state)
      return PRUNED_SLOTNAME
    return None

`

I would suggest *not* creating a tutorial directory.  This is a lesson learned from boto, where these service specific tutorials never get fleshed out.

Instead I would recommend creating a dynamodb guide that goes under the pre-existing guide/ directory.  We would only have service guides for things that have high level abstractions.

I think eventually we'll make the existing tutorial more generic and less SQS specific, but I would not advise per-service tutorials.

And finally, we should also somehow show the expected output of each call.  We can filter out things like request ids, etc, but I think it's important for the user to see the expected output so they have some indication if they're getting the same results the tutorial gets when they call the various methods.

import dynet_config

    dy.GRUBuilder(1, 10, 7, model)

    outs = [dy.transpose(dy.transpose(hc) * V) + b for hc in hidden_c]

I haven't gotten into this in great depth yet, but I'd like to say that the _existing_ AWS provisioning process can create a VPC with two subnets-- each in a different availability zone.  It would be bad to lose that ability.

> Multi-AZ is extremely complicated and I think that should be beyond the scope of this.

Multi-_region_ is complicated.  I do think _that_ should be beyond the scope, but multi-_AZ_ is an absolute necessity for anyone wishing to guarantee the availability of the platform and the apps deployed to the platform.

> the problem with multi-AZ is that if connectivity drops between the AZs for any reason, you can easily end up with a split brain problem that can be very difficult to resolve

This can also happen _within_ a single AZ if nodes are unable to talk to one another for any reason, so it might not be impetus not to do multi-AZ.

> and it still probably should require 3 AZs

Yes.  At least three are required if you want to guarantee that an AZ-wide problem in an AZ hosting the majority of your nodes doesn't force you to lose quorum.  By having at least three AZs, you can avoid any one having a _majority_ of your nodes.

> Also, there's still the issue of backend data storage and which AZ that's in, how to route the DB requests to the appropriate DB, etc.

This isn't a problem for those running the "stateless platform" (ceph-less), and opting to use RDS or similar for storage.  This seems to be an increasingly common choice among those concerned about minimizing the platform's own moving parts and maximizing availability.

> Making sure overlay is loaded on boot seems actionable. New issue?

I second @jchauncey . It's an easy trick which simplify the implementation but degrade the dev experience.

Also I didn't understand why the heathchecker should knock only once for startup. Why not keeping it for the whole life of the node, reporting healthiness for all running services ?

It can be something simple inspired from the gossip protocols: each second, each life-checker of each node chose a random service from the whole cluster, and check its liveliness. It then update the correct key in etcd with last time update and status. (it's the way [Serf](serfdom.io) works with membership).

We need to run the numbers to see when statistically we would hit a new service considering different coreos configurations. But a prioritization can also be done: the new-service list is prioritized against the list of already running services. This way we know sooner when they are up without degrading the global health-checking too much.

Last but not least, it would be nice to have more than one healthcheck point. The configuration can be trivial: the `GET` request on the healthcheck endpoint _can_ return one or more `Location:` headers, which would then be checked. That one would be neat.

For the record, pip does this by automating the vendoring provess. It contains an equivalent of requirements.txt (`pip/_vendor/vendor.txt`) and a script to automatically pull in packages based on the specified version (`pip/vendor/__init__.py`). It also manages vendored (and patched) libraries very well to make sure they are as up to date as possible.

@techalchemy 

> The warehouse json api posts the most recent dependencies even if you’re dealing with an old version, and that’s if it has that info at all

This just plain wrong, you can get a specific version dependencies by calling `https://pypi.org/pypi/{project}/{release}/json`. If you just call `https://pypi.org/pypi/{project}/json` sure you will only get the last dependencies but you can actually get the right set of dependencies.

And the packaging/publishing part of python projects really need to be improved because in the end it will benefit everyone, since it will make it possible to use the JSON API reliably.

> It manages idempotent environments and generates a reproducible lockfile.
> We resolve lockfiles and include markers for python versions that you don’t have, aren’t using, and keep that available so that you can precisely deploy and reproduce across platforms and python versions.

And so does `poetry`. And you can make it not use the JSON API to provide the use the same resolution method as `pipenv` (using pip-tools). See https://github.com/sdispater/poetry/issues/37#issuecomment-379071989 and it will still be more resilient than `pipenv` (https://github.com/sdispater/poetry#dependency-resolution)

I thought it would be impossible to tell Travis to upload (deploy) a wheel built using the manylinux1 docker container. However, I've figured out how to do that now using some bash commands, and it works like a charm. Something like:

    # (build 32-bit and 64-bit wheels using the manylinux1 docker container)
    # ...
    find dist/ -name "*.whl" -exec auditwheel repair {} \;
    rm dist/*
    mv wheelhouse/*.whl dist/

Then I set `distributions: sdist` (building only the source distribution) and `skip_cleanup: true` and in the `.travis.yml` deploy section. Based on the Travis docs, I think that the `skip_cleanup` prevent Travis from deleting/overwriting the `dist/` directory, so that all wheels previously built with the docker container are uploaded to PyPI. You can take a look at the code [here](https://github.com/flo-compbio/xlmhg) (the relevant scripts are in the tools/ directory).

As suggested in the changelog, I'm reporting this change as a regression. It seems to break pip editable (-e) installs if there's already a non-editable install of the same package. To reproduce:

1. create a virtualenv and make sure it contains setuptools 25.0.0 or later
2. pip install something from PyPI
3. pip install -e a local source tree of the same software

You'll end up with the package you installed in step 2 rather than the editable version from step 3. (Note that if you use pip install without -e things still work properly and you end up with the version from step 3 instead.)

For example, the old (working) behavior is demonstrated by:
```
git clone git://git.openstack.org/openstack/openstackdocstheme.git
virtualenv foo
./foo/bin/pip install setuptools==24.3.1
./foo/bin/pip install openstackdocstheme==1.5.0
./foo/bin/pip install -e ./openstackdocstheme
./foo/bin/pip list|grep openstackdocstheme
```
You should see something like openstackdocstheme 1.5.1.dev1 referencing a path to the source directory you just installed.

Now blow away the `foo` virtualenv and repeat except use `setuptools==25.0.0` (or any later version). You'll see that instead the result is that you still have openstackdocstheme 1.5.0 referencing the `foo` virtualenv's site-packages directory instead of the editable install.

What @tjprescott said. For ARM control plane operations, the platform is generally using PUT as the only means for resource creates. Which is inherently idempotent. Which means that our create commands are expected to be idempotent. Which also has the benefit of ensuring that the resource not only exists, but that it is also in the state you expect (e.g. having a network resource in the wrong location probably won't help - you probably want your script to fail or correct the existing resource at that point). The main drawback is performance for some resources (in theory, it should be a no-op if the resource already exists in the state indicated by the request - in practice, less so). 

The pattern we originally wanted to follow was to have a 'create if not exists' flag on the create command. However, unfortunately, it has never been a requirement for an Azure resource provider to provide support for etags/if-match/if-none-match headers, which means that this support is somewhat spotty (we are pushing to get it included in the next rev of the Azure Resource Provider contract - not sure if it will make it or not). We could (and still can) implement this with a GET to determine if the resource exists beforehand modulo the race condition introduced if the resource provider doesn't support etags/if-non-match. 

Thoughts?

Hi Anton,

My understanding is that bcolz does not provide any kind of write
synchronization. If you want to append from multiple processes then you
would need to manage some kind of lock in your own application. If all your
processes are spawned from a single Python process via the multiprocessing
module then one option may be to use the multiprocessing.Lock class.
Alternatively there is a 3rd party Python package called fasteners [1]
which provides an inter-process lock based on file locking, which may be a
more flexible option.

Hth,
Alistair

[1] https://fasteners.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/process_lock.html

The Kinto server is an HTTP service, it works with every browser.

The kinto.js client is absolutely not necessary to use Kinto in a JavaScript application. Using Angular or pure XmlHttpRequest would also work perfectly! 

kinto.js is relevant to provide offline/sync, and relies on IndexedDB, promises etc. to do it. Since it provides the polyfills to run on not-so-modern browsers, it should be ok with IE11. If you have a particular problem with kinto.js on the browser you mention, I suggest that you open an issue on the kinto.js repo instead.

I'm closing this, but feel free to re-open if you still have some doubts !

Update on  https://michielbdejong.com/kinto-buckets.html - it was still failing after `Cache-Control: no-cache` was added to kinto.dev, but that was because there was a bug in my test script (not setting `dbPrefix: userid`) which is fixed now. So I think for browser cache (and probably also for shared-cache proxies, because they should also revalidate), it's now safe to use the default bucket on kinto.dev. A revalidate will always be triggered, and then Kinto will do two things:

* check the Authorization header
* respond with the ETag for the user corresponding to the Authorization header, so the cache knows which cached data to serve, based on that ETag header, and will never serve the wrong data.

Here's one suggested way to implement this:

Pick some special character not currently allowed in package names to designate this, and literally name the package `py:bokeh-0.7.1-np19py33_0.tar.bz2`, and have conda manage this internally. 

Upsides of this idea: 

- Quite easy to implement. Currently packages are indexed by their filename, so allowing two packages with the same filename to be different is a big no no. 

- Makes it clear that this distinction is at the UI level (or rather, at the spec level). 

- Since it is only a change at the spec level, no lower level code will need to be changed.

- Should be backwards compatible, minus the UI conveniences. We should probably require dependency specs to specify the full name in order to guarantee this (plus that's a good idea anyway).

Downsides:

- Some packages (like bokeh) already exist, and are named normally, so we'd have to somehow maintain backwards compatibility with them. And I think just deprecating the old way is not good enough, because this sort of thing can happen to any package. I wouldn't want to require every package to have this, though, or even necessarily recommend it.

- The list of potential characters isn't so hot. Windows reserves `<`, `>`, `:`, `"`, `/`, `\`, `|`, `?`, and `*`.  If we want to use the same character in the file name as at the command line, it's harder, because some characters are bad to use because they would have to be quoted in most shells (such as bash), including `>`, `;`, `#`, `$`, `&`, `"`, and `'`. `=` is already used at the command line.  The character we use pretty much has to be a typable ASCII character.

Good choices that remain (that I can think of): `bokeh~py`, `bokeh(py)`, `bokeh[py]`, `bokeh%py`, `bokeh,py` (or the same with the `py` first). Please bikeshed on this.

- Technically speaking, this changes the package spec.

  - anaconda-fusion
  - defaults

@davidslac , In conda 4.0.x the BINSTAR_TOKENS_PAT is always a global variable, but in 4.1.x, it's still a global variable but defined in init_binstar() function. When the remove_binstar_tokens is called before init_binstar, you will get this issue. It will definitely fixed in future.

@vpatryshev "I guess", sorry Vlad I can't go with just I guess :-) The problem is that H2O's and Spark MLlib's APIs/approaches are a bit different, don't think it's a Scala problem but more a Spark one:

1) Spark's algorithms work *only* on numeric vectors (so they are ok with `Vector[Double]`) whereas H2O's algorithms can work on different datatypes (String, Int, Double) for the same algorithm
2) Spark's algorithms have all features in one column which contains `Vector[Double]`, whereas H2O's algorithms work on a Frame which has each feature in a separate column

Personally in SW I'd prefer to have an API for our wrappers which is consistent with MLlib's API even if it means a small hack.

This isn't a language (Scala) problem, since in Scala I could simply make a `Array[Any]`, which is not a supported type for a column in Spark (not sure why, might be because Tungsten wouldn't be able to optimize it, not sure).

There are 2 approaches I see:

1) go with the approach I'm doing here, a bit hacky but in line with Spark's API
2) mix both approaches, use `featureColumns(Array[String])` in H2O wrappers which resembles Spark's API but uses multiple columns instead of one and use those columns for training/predicting.

Or I'm open to other solutions but they have to be generic and user friendly, not a case-by-case solutions.

It appears you have MyClass6 defined both in Scala and Python.  Therefore when you call the constructor in Python `mc6 = MyClass6()` you create a Python MyClass6 since you didn't import the Java/Scala one.  In the current versions of Jep you can't return a Python object to Java/Scala.  Jep will attempt to auto-convert common Python objects like primitives, strings, lists, and dictionaries to Java equivalents, but the fallback behavior is to return the `str()` version of the Python object (which is less than ideal but it's been the behavior in Jep for years).  In short, you can't get a Python object out of `getValue(String)` unless Jep has already coded the conversion.  We are looking at returning Python objects (or at least pointers to them) to Java for Jep 4.0 but that is experimental and not guaranteed.

I'm not quite sure what you're aiming for, but if you just have the Java/Scala class, in the Python you should be able to do `from fullyQualifiedPackageName import MyClass6` and you'll have the Java/Scala class wrapped in a Python object.  So remove the Python definition of MyClass6, in Python import the Java/Scala version, and then the rest of your code should work.

Practically speaking, logargW is determined by Rsh*IL, which can be expected to be <10^6, so order<6.  Now that I think about it, I’d just skip this whole business and set range(0,8). We get roughly 2 digits in base 10 of precision with each iteration, so 9 iterations should get us double precision (53 bits) for the mantissa of w.

In Matlab, we only do the logspace calculations where argW is too large.  That filtering is not present in the python version; we calculate the logspace values for all inputs. If there’s need for a performance improvement, maybe that filter would help.

I’d rather not use scipy.optimize.newton in this instance because its stopping criteria is an absolute (not relative) tolerance on w (or a maximum number of iterations).  We want precision across such a large range of magnitudes that the tolerance has to scale with the argument, and it seemed simpler to scale the iteration count.  I also prefer explicit code over package references, but that’s more of a Matlab mindset I’m projecting onto python.

In any case, this still needs a call to np.max to handle array-like input. It's probably most efficient to make np.max the first function call: np.ceil(np.log10(np.max(w))).astype(int)

Do we need to set a minimum order value? If all w are less than 1, all log10(w) will be negative, and all ceil will return 0 or less. order = np.maximum(order, 1) might work for that.

I also think there should be a maximum order value so that we don't end up in a near infinite loop for erroneous input. order = np.minimum(order, 10) might work, though I don't know what value to recommend.

Or we could avoid all of the above by following @mikofski<https://github.com/mikofski>'s suggestion to use one of the scipy.optimize routines. I still think we have to separately call the routine on each element of array-like input, though. Wrapping the routine with np.vectorize might be ideal since it would take care of broadcasting for us.

re: name(s), label(s), shape(s):

For the purpose of this discussion "multi-valued" means a parameter that provides values to go in multiple `DataArray`s - so NOT if we reinstate case 3, a parameter returning a single array value.

- all parameters need a `name`. A multi-valued parameter also needs `names`, a sequence of strings to give names to each array.

- single valued parameters have `label`, multi-valued parameters have `labels`, a sequence of strings.

- multi-valued parameters have `shapes`, a sequence of shape tuples. There is no `shape` unless we reinstate case 3.

So from a consistency standpoint, `unit` and `units` is correct, but from a standpoint of "make simple things simple," I feel like I'd always be messing up and typing `units` in the simple case because that's what I always think of it being called.

@niklasl is right, the issue is that `2.2` in turtle is datatype decimal, whereas `2.2e0` is a double. Since we have a double, we either have to serialize as a string with explicit datatype, or using the `e` notation. 

We do the serialisation using `"%e"%myfloat` notation, where Python will default to 6 digits of precision. 

For the original posters literal we get the "right" value with 11 digits: 

```py
> '%.11e'%14.0030740048
'1.40030740048e+01'
```

BUT, there is no way to know how much precision to include, since this information is lost in a float/double. 

My take would be `xsd:double` and `xsd:float` are intended to be represented as a floating point value internally, with all the trouble this bring with it. 

If you need higher precision you can use `xsd:decimal` (which maps to the Decimal class in python. These should keep your the same precision that you input, and you can also make your json-ld save literals with this data-type explicitly. 

See also: https://www.w3.org/TR/swbp-xsch-datatypes/#sec-numerics

> Would be possible to write a Python script and build a Data Table?

Yes, that's possible. This is far from ideal but here is a simple example on how to achieve this in the current version. Open the Python Script widget and paste this code:
```py
import os
import numpy as np
from Orange.data.domain import Domain, StringVariable
from orangecontrib.text.corpus import Corpus


PATH = '<the-path-to-the-group-folder>'
MAX_DOC = 1000

# create Orange's domain; all features will be in metas
meta_attrs = [StringVariable('group'), StringVariable('year'), StringVariable('filename'), StringVariable('content')]
domain = Domain(attributes=[], class_vars=[], metas=meta_attrs)

# create numpy array for storing the data
data = np.empty((MAX_DOC, 4), dtype=object)
doc_ind = 0

class BreakIt(Exception): pass    # enable stopping
try:
    for dir, _, files in os.walk(PATH):                # walk through all sub-directories
        for file in files:                             # and through all files inside sub-directories
            if file.endswith('.txt'):                  # if the type matches
                group, year = dir.split(os.sep)[-2:]   # parse group and year

                # read file's content and delete \n characters
                with open(os.path.join(dir, file), 'r') as f:
                    content = f.read().replace('\n', ' ')
            
                # store the data to numpy matrix    
                data[doc_ind, 0] = group
                data[doc_ind, 1] = year
                data[doc_ind, 2] = file
                data[doc_ind, 3] = content
                doc_ind += 1
        
                if doc_ind == MAX_DOC:    # if you loaded enough, stop
                    raise BreakIt
except BreakIt:
    pass
                
# truncate empty lines
data = data[:doc_ind, :]
print('Loaded {} documents.'.format(doc_ind))

# create corpus
corp = Corpus.from_numpy(domain, X=np.empty((doc_ind, 0)), metas=data)
# set which features to use for text mining; use only content
corp.set_text_features(meta_attrs[-1:])

# send the data to the output
out_object = corp
```

What this does is walks through the folder hierarchy and loads all txt files. All you have to set is the `PATH` variable at the top of the script, which should point to the root `groups` folder. Note that there is also a `MAX_DOC` variable that stops loading after `MAX_DOC` number of documents have been loaded.

Once you load the documents using this code a Corpus will be present on the output signal `out_object` of the widget. You can connect it to other text widgets as you would normally do, just make sure that you are using `out_object` and not `out_data` (this could be changed by clicking on the line between the two widgets).

I should also warn you that you might encounter some memory issues when trying to load 3.5 GB data set into Orange. I suggest you start with some small subset of documents. Also let us know, how this will go.

The IP address does not need to be supplied with calls to the REST API - PayPal will get the sender's IP address from the incoming request automatically.

`DoDirectPayment` is part of the [Merchant API](https://developer.paypal.com/docs/classic/api/merchant/DoDirectPayment_API_Operation_SOAP/), one of PayPal's [Classic APIs](https://developer.paypal.com/docs/classic/).  This SDK is built on PayPal's [REST APIs](https://developer.paypal.com/docs/api/).  The AVS and CVV response codes are not accessible via API calls at this time.  In the event of a validation failure, you may receive an error response that includes more specific information.  Concerning an AVS failure, there's currently a [known issue](https://github.com/paypal/PayPal-NET-SDK/issues/61#issuecomment-68601319) where the specific AVS error code isn't being returned and a generic error message is being returned instead.  The API team is aware of this issue and is working on a solution.

> Feels like we should consider a flag, something like --verbose-package-output, in case someone wants to see the details, particularly in the case of debugging an issue.

Sure, I'll add it for folks who want to live the verbose lifestyle.

I'm not sure how useful it'll be for debugging in practice with typical usage. If you're running `new` normally without the verbose flag, when you do run into a package restore failure, it'd be easier to just rerun the restore command itself manually, rather than rerunning `new` with a verbose flag because running `new` again will first fail due to existing files it just created (requiring `--force` to overwrite), and will then walk through creating a new project (i.e. choosing project name, description, stack name, config, etc.), which you don't really want because you already did all that.

To make it easier to rerun just the package restore command, it'd be simplest to just output the command we ran upon failure, e.g. `error: installing dependencies; rerun 'pip install -r requirements.txt' manually to try again`.

Of course we could consider making `new` smarter to notice when there's an existing `Pulumi.yaml` and currently selected stack, and offer to skip some of the project creation steps. Or have an `--install-dependencies-only` command that just runs the package restore command. I prefer the verbose flag and outputting the command to start with, and we can consider some of these other ideas if there's an actual need.

The problem appears to come about because of these lines in ``google_adapters.py``:

```python
if args_get('limitby', None):
    (lmin, lmax) = attributes['limitby']
    limit, fetch_args = lmax-lmin, {'offset':lmin,'keys_only':True}
```

The adapter sets 'keys_only' on an intermediate query to implement 'limitby'. I have not studied GAE's various databases, so I don't know whether or not this is the only way to get the 'limitby' functionality. But since, in the short term, I will not be suggesting anything that requires changes to ``google_adapters.py``, let's assume 'keys_only' is necessary in the adapter.

So, some choices I see which may solve your problem:

1) If you don't need 'limitby', you could remove the 'limitby' and have the query work.

2) If you are using the 'projection' attribute for attempted performance improvements, you could remove 'projection' and have the query work.

3) If you were using the 'projection' attribute to trim the results to just the selected fields, there is a work around: You can use the 'filterfields' attribute instead of the 'projection' attribute.

4) You mentioned using GAE locally. I assume this is because you intend to target GAE remotely.  If this is not so, then there are other databases which are more capable and a little better supported by pydal and web2py.

Editorial Note:

For case 3), I consider the *need* to use the 'filterfields' attribute a bug in ``google_adapters.py``, or at least an ill-advised design choice. Most (all?) of the other adapters return only the selected fields. If the user went to the trouble to select fields in the query, why would they ever want additional fields in the response?

Since the struct name is required for the autocompleter to work, changing what Metakernel thinks a word boundary is won't work, so I'll have to simply have do_matlab_complete.m parse the input string for a '.' then make sure to output the correct string in that case.

Just had a conversation with a bunch of us around the office about how we want to save ECDSA keys under `/etc/letsencrypt`. The proposal we came up with is the following:

Allow users to have a single lineage that contains both ECDSA and RSA keys. This lineage will store all 8 symlinks (`cert.pem`, `privkey.pem`,...,`cert-ecdsa.pem`, `privkey-ecdsa.pem`,...) under `/etc/letsencrypt/live/lineagename`. Modifications to the lineage affect both the ECDSA and RSA certificates.

We're not planning to make changes to this directory atomic (they aren't right now either). The way things currently work is files are written to `/etc/letsencrypt/archive` first and only after that is complete are the symlinks in `/etc/letsencrypt/live` updated. We then have recovery logic that fix up the symlinks as needed on the next client run. This process can be fixed up to work with lineages with both key types for the time being.

We will also have to store more information in the renewal configuration file. For example, we currently don't save the domains in the lineage, treating the current certificate as the ground truth and reading the domains from that. This process doesn't work well if there are multiple certificates in the lineage because the names between the certs can differ if the client crashes. Such information will have to be saved in the renewal configuration file. When this file is updated with respect to the symlinks in the `live` directory is an implementation detail that has to be figured out.

Other things that were discussed include:
* Having the order they key types are requested by the user affect which certificate is obtained/renewed first by Certbot.
* Reusing certificates in the `archive` directory if we crash after obtaining one certificate.
* Reorganizing the `/etc/letsencrypt` directory to allow for more atomicity between updates.

I think all of these things are nice to have, but not essential to initially support ECDSA keys. I believe @schoen plans to work on implementing some of this in the next couple months so we can merge ECDSA support into the client relatively soon.

I don't know if this bug is still being worked on or is still relevant, but for the benefit of anyone who runs into it my workaround is:

Generate initial certificate using: `certbot certonly --webroot -w /path/to/document_root -d example.org`

Create a cronjob with the following content to automatically renew certificates and then reload the web server so that it picks up the new certs:

```
set -u
set -e

/usr/bin/certbot renew
/usr/sbin/service nginx reload
```

I've been running the above for several months now, including through multiple renewal cycles, without any problems.

I may have some insight here, having just run into the issue myself, and it doesn't require a massively-overloaded server.

With Curator's switchover to using the bulk API instead of acting on individual indices, operations, such as the bulk deletion of a bunch of huge indices, will take place over a single long-lived request, which can timeout.  Bumping up the --timeout option fixes this on the client side... but not on the server side.  I executed a bulk deletion via Curator while tailing the master node's logs, and saw this:

```
[2015-07-16 14:10:49,269][INFO ][cluster.metadata         ] [logstashsearch103-master-01] [common-2015.04.30] deleting index
[2015-07-16 14:10:59,057][INFO ][cluster.metadata         ] [logstashsearch103-master-01] [common-2015.05.01] deleting index
[2015-07-16 14:11:05,813][INFO ][cluster.metadata         ] [logstashsearch103-master-01] [common-2015.05.02] deleting index
[2015-07-16 14:11:06,932][INFO ][cluster.metadata         ] [logstashsearch103-master-01] [common-2015.05.03] deleting index
[2015-07-16 14:11:07,898][INFO ][cluster.metadata         ] [logstashsearch103-master-01] [common-2015.05.04] deleting index
[2015-07-16 14:11:19,269][DEBUG][action.admin.indices.delete] [logstashsearch103-master-01] [common-2015.05.05] failed to delete index
org.elasticsearch.cluster.metadata.ProcessClusterEventTimeoutException: failed to process cluster event (delete-index [common-2015.05.05]) within 30s
        at org.elasticsearch.cluster.service.InternalClusterService$2$1.run(InternalClusterService.java:278)
        at java.util.concurrent.ThreadPoolExecutor.runWorker(ThreadPoolExecutor.java:1142)
        at java.util.concurrent.ThreadPoolExecutor$Worker.run(ThreadPoolExecutor.java:617)
        at java.lang.Thread.run(Thread.java:745)
[2015-07-16 14:11:19,272][DEBUG][action.admin.indices.delete] [logstashsearch103-master-01] [common-2015.05.09] failed to delete index
org.elasticsearch.cluster.metadata.ProcessClusterEventTimeoutException: failed to process cluster event (delete-index [common-2015.05.09]) within 30s
        at org.elasticsearch.cluster.service.InternalClusterService$2$1.run(InternalClusterService.java:278)
        at java.util.concurrent.ThreadPoolExecutor.runWorker(ThreadPoolExecutor.java:1142)
        at java.util.concurrent.ThreadPoolExecutor$Worker.run(ThreadPoolExecutor.java:617)
        at java.lang.Thread.run(Thread.java:745)
```

So, --timeout extends the timeout for the *client* side, but the *server* side is unaffected.  You get a success message on the client side, presumably because you get a response from the server within the specified timeframe, but sadly the response is a timeout message.  It's likely, of course, that *some* of the indices you requested to be deleted were actually deleted, but probably not all of them.

Fortunately, I think there should be a trivial solution here: you can extend the server-side timeout for these operations with the `master_timeout=(value)` GET string argument.  If this were set and inherited the value passed from --timeout, I believe this would resolve this issue.

OK, I did have to resort to using `requests` package to get this done reliably. The code below replaces `_fetch_file()` instead of altering url_open. Obviously it's a much bigger change than I was aiming for before, so I haven't done the whole job and made the PR.

Just to say though that switching to use the `requests` package would help in various ways because:

- it becomes Py3/Py2 compatible without you having to jump through extra hoops
- it's more robust then the multiple built-in options
- it handles streaming chunk iteration for you (no need for your additional functions to do that)

So, it would be a fair bit of refactoring, but I think you'd get something more robust by having the right tool rather than writing requests code yourself! :-)

For my own purposes I'll use a monkey patch, so I don't mind either way!

```
def _fetch_file(url, file_name, print_destination=True, chunk_size=8192):
    """Load requested file, downloading it if needed or requested

    Parameters
    ----------
    url: string
        The url of file to be downloaded.
    file_name: string
        Name, along with the path, of where downloaded file will be saved.
    print_destination: bool, optional
        If true, destination of where file was saved will be printed after
        download finishes.
    resume: bool, optional
        If true, try to resume partially downloaded files.
    """
    # Adapted from NISL:
    # https://github.com/nisl/tutorial/blob/master/nisl/datasets.py
    
    print('Imageio: %r was not found on your computer; '
          'downloading it now.' % os.path.basename(file_name))
    
    temp_file_name = file_name + ".part"
    local_file = None
    initial_size = 0
    errors = []
    with requests.get(url, timeout=5.0, stream=True) as remote_file:
        file_size = int(remote_file.headers['Content-Length'].strip())
        size_str = _sizeof_fmt(file_size)
        print('Try %i. Download from %s (%s)' % (tries+1, url, size_str))
        #create progress bar
        progress = StdoutProgressIndicator('Downloading')
        progress.start('', 'bytes', file_size)
        with open(temp_file_name, 'wb') as fd:
            for chunk in remote_file.iter_content(chunk_size=chunk_size):
                fd.write(chunk)
                progress.increase_progress(len(chunk))
        shutil.move(temp_file_name, file_name)
        if print_destination is True:
            sys.stdout.write('File saved as %s.\n' % file_name)
        break
    progress.finish('Done')
```

Thanks @takluyver.  I'm pretty sure I see what's going on here - adding @minrk and @parente for comment.

Once the `json_errors` decorator was removed, this caused the `JSONErrorsMixin.write_error` to be called in JKG instead of `APIHandler.write_error` due to inheritance rules.  However, `JSONErrorsMixin` doesn't deal with tracebacks and essentially ignores any non-HTTP kinds of exceptions - like NoSuchKernel - leading to test failures.

Since `APIHandler` is in the handler class hierarchy of JKG classes, it stands to reason that we could now get rid of `JSONErrorsMixin.write_error` now that `APIHandler.write_error` exists and handles both HTTP and non-HTTP exceptions.  However, there's one issue that I believe was an oversight in the #2853 and that's that `APIHandler.write_error` does not set the `reason` for the reply, whereas `@json_errors` did that before.

I stumbled upon this when looking at another test failure where the reason field for a 403 exception was not the expected `Forbidden` but instead `None`.  In looking at `JSONErrorsMixin` it makes the following call: `reason = responses.get(status_code, 'Unknown HTTP Error')`.  But, `APIHandler.write_error` essentially does the equivalent when initializing the `message` field, only to overwrite that field with info from the exc_info structure (if it exists).  As a result, I need to ask if `APIHandler.write_error` should be using `reason` where `message` is used in these lines: https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/base/handlers.py#L461-L464

If I make that change, then all JKG tests pass when `JSONErrorsMixin` is removed from the class hierarchy.

The other approach is to apply the content of `APIHandler.write_error` - along with the "reason update" I raised - directly into `JSONErrorsMixin.write_error` and just live with retaining `JSONErrorsMixin` in JKG.  I think we'll need to do this anyway in order to bridge this gap from a release perspective in the near term anyway.

Although this issue is long dead, I'll just mention that this ability (using a callable for tags) is essential for allowing the two-pass approach to validation mention in #109. By using a callable that always returns `True` for both tags and attributes, you can provoke bleach to produce unfiltered output to compare to the cleaned output.

Without this, you have to think of and list every possible tag that might exist, now and in the future. That approach is too brittle.

I understand that validation is not a goal of this project, but I'll just mention here for others who are interested that with a few lines of code (including the change in this PR) you can turn bleach into an effective validator.

No -- the difference is in how the models are evaluated, **not accuracy**. We report all accuracy for a single-crop with the size of the receptive field (in this case 224x224). This keeps comparisons between models fair. (InceptionV3 is evaluated on a 299x299 image, since it has a larger receptive field).

Caffe (and the VGG paper) report multi-scale evaluation to get to the 7.5% number. Even their single-scale evaluation is done at larger resolution (256x256). This is more computationally expensive. If you evaluate the PyTorch pre-trained models at the larger scale, you'll get better results too.

Initialization from VGG-C doesn't matter -- you just need a good learning rate schedule.

The most common interpretation of this would be to use the user’s given name as the “short” identifier, and the user’s full name as the “long” identifier. Hi,

If the bcl2fastq cannot write the InterOp file, then in some versions it will stop with an error and others it will just log a warning.

Two or more bcl2fastq jobs will not communicate with each other, so likely the output InterOp and stats file (if all the jobs are pointed at the same output folder) will just be corrupted.

You could use the InterOp library to combine all the InterOp files into a single file (assuming all the bcl2fastq jobs are pointing to different output directories).

The % aligned data is produced by RTA not bcl2fastq. So you will not find the data in the bcl2fastq outputs.

Also, the InterOp library only reads InterOp files (RunInfo.xml and RunParameters.xml), not the XML or Json files produced by bcl2fastq.

Hope this helps.

Hi Dave,

I think this issue stems from us installing an Anaconda distribution of Python by default in the notebook initialization script and using that to run the notebooks. When you pip install a package from the notebook, it uses the Anaconda distribution's pip, but I'm guessing when you were using pip install in your own init script, it was using a different pip and in the notebooks the Anaconda Python couldn't see that installation of the package. 

Regardless, I've moved away from using Anaconda by default in #31 and instead using the default system Python. I've also added a `--pkgs` flag to the `cluster start`. You should be able to specify packages to install with pip either using that flag, i.e. `cluster start mycluster --pkgs pandas,google-cloud-storage`, or by pip installing from your own init script supplied with `--init`.

Thanks,
Liam

Hi all,
The problem seems to be in line 43 of packager.py.
```python
self.upload = upload or os.getenv("CONAN_UPLOAD", None)
```
upload is a boolean but os.getenv("CONAN_UPLOAD", None) is a string if you set the environment variable, so self.upload under this circumstances is always a string if upload == False

Then in line 342 in method "_upload_packages" you can see:
```python
if not self.upload:
    return
```

As I said self.upload is a string so `not "some string"` always returns False and never enter in that if statement. That is the reason why it always uploads packages even if you set the environment variable to False.

As a solution changing the line 43 by:
 ```python
self.upload = upload or (os.getenv("CONAN_UPLOAD", None) in ["True", "true", "1"]
```
May fix this issue.

Try installing a version of pymavlink prior to 2.0.

The attribute callbacks are indeed called from a separate thread, because they are triggered by MAVLink message listeners ([example](https://github.com/dronekit/dronekit-python/blob/master/dronekit/__init__.py#L1072)), which in turn are called from the [`mavlink_thread_in`](https://github.com/dronekit/dronekit-python/blob/master/dronekit/mavlink.py#L231) function, executed in a separate thread.

You should never do long-running tasks in those callbacks, otherwise you'll block the main MAVLink receiving thread.

Hi, anything that's after the `*` is part of the projection that's created from `*`.  Conceptually, it's parsed as `a.*.(c)` and `a.*.(c.d)`.  So breaking that down, we have:

```
>>> jmespath.search("a", {"a": { "b": { "c": { "d": "AAAA"}}}})
{'b': {'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}}
>>> jmespath.search("a.*", {"a": { "b": { "c": { "d": "AAAA"}}}})
[{'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}]
```

So for `a.*.c`, it's evaluating the expression `c` against each element in the list of `[{'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}]`:

```
[{'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}]   --> search('c') -> {'d': 'AAAA'}

# So the final result is a list of each search result:

[{'d': 'AAAA'}]
```

That means that `a.*.c.d` would execute as this:

```
[{'c': {'d': 'AAAA'}}]   --> search('c.d') -> None

# And in a projection, a `None` value is excluded from the resulting list,
# so the final result would be an empty list:
[]
```

More info about that here: http://jmespath.org/specification.html#wildcard-expressions

Let me know if any of that isn't clear or you have any more questions.

`scout_apm.api.instrument(name, tags={}, kind="Custom")`

* `name` - A semi-detailed version of what the section of code is. It should be static between different invocations of the method. Individual details like user id, or counts or other data points can be added as tags. Names like `retreive_from_api` or `GET` are good names.
* `kind` - A high level area of the application. This defaults to `Custom`. Your whole application should have a very low number of unique strings here. In our built-in instruments, this is things like `Template` and `SQL`. For custom instrumentation, it can be strings like `MongoDB` or `HTTP` or similar. This should not change based on input or state of the application.
* `tags` - A dictionary of key/value pairs. Key should be a string, but value can be any json-able structure.

## As a Context Manager:

Wrap a section of code in a unique "span" of work.

The yielded object can be used to add additional tags individually.

```python
def foo():
  with scout_apm.api.instrument("Computation 1") as instrument:
    instrument.tag("record_count", 100)
    # Work

  with scout_apm.api.instrument("Computation 2", tags: { 'filtered_record_count: 50 } ) as instrument:  
    # Work
```

## As a decorator:

Wraps a whole function in a custom trace. Same API as the ContextManager style.

```python
@scout_apm.api.instrument("Computation", tags: { 'x': 1 })
def bar():
  # Work
```

These aren't changing anything to do with what is returned. Exceptions are not 'returned', there is a completely different system in Python for propagating exceptions.

The [`raise` statement in Python](https://docs.python.org/2.6/reference/simple_stmts.html#the-raise-statement) takes three expressions. If any of them are omitted then `None` in used in their place. If the third expression is `None` then it uses the current frame to get the stacktrace. Explicitly setting it to `sys.exc_info()[2]` means that it will instead use the stacktrace from where the captured exception was raised.

Effectively, it is changing:
```python
raise JobError("Error: %s" % str(err)), None, None
```
to
```python
raise JobError("Error: %s" % str(err)), None, sys.exc_info()[2]
```

Here's what I currently know:

**Regarding option 1)**

From Rail 2.1 rail_types.h (Rail 2.0 doesn't define RAIL_EVENTS_TX_COMPLETION)

```
/**
 * This mask indicates all of the events that determine the end of a transmitted
 * packet. After a packet has begun to be transmitted, which is indicated by
 * a \ref RAIL_STATUS_NO_ERROR return value from one of the transmit functions,
 * exactly one of the following events will occur. When one of these events
 * occurs a state transition will take place based on the parameter
 * passed to RAIL_SetTxTransitions(). The RAIL_StateTransitions_t::success
 * transition will be followed only if the \ref RAIL_EVENT_TX_PACKET_SENT
 * event occurs. Any of the other events will trigger the
 * RAIL_StateTransitions_t::error transition.
 */
#define RAIL_EVENTS_TX_COMPLETION (RAIL_EVENT_TX_PACKET_SENT \
                                   | RAIL_EVENT_TX_ABORTED   \
                                   | RAIL_EVENT_TX_BLOCKED   \
                                   | RAIL_EVENT_TX_UNDERFLOW \
                                   | RAIL_EVENT_TX_CHANNEL_BUSY)
```

We currently handle all of these and on every one of them set `sTransmitBusy = false;`. The only exception is when we receive RAIL_EVENT_TX_PACKET_SENT where we check for `(sTransmitFrame.mPsdu[0] & IEEE802154_ACK_REQUEST) == 0`. 

I checked and in every single case, the packet that gets stuck in transmission does not require an ACK. (It's a bit strange actually - never saw a packet that requires an ACK to get stuck).

**Regarding option 2)**

When the issue occurs, I print RAIL_GetRadioState(sRailHandle) every second and it's always equal to RAIL_RF_STATE_TX. Not completely sure how rail works in this regard, but I'd expect the radio to switch to reception or idle mode upon failed TX.


If it's actually a bug in Rail then it might be difficult to relay it to SiLabs as it's quite difficult to reproduce and we don't have Rail source code.

> Ah, I misunderstood, sorry. It should still be possible to work incrementally and start pinning versions of dependencies that you need to stop varying as they occur

As far as I can tell, there is no sane way to know when a transitive-only dep varies. FWICT, the process would go like this:
1. pin all direct dependencies by editing `requirements.txt` files slurped up by `python_requirements` targets as well as any `python_reuirement_library` BUILD targets.
2. run some pants goal that triggers requirement resolution, in this case `binary` sounds appropriate, but run with PEX_VERBOSE=5
3. stare at the output to see which (transitive-only) deps specify a version range as opposed to an exact requirement.
4. for each of those, add a Pants `python_requirement_library` target or line to a `requirements.txt` that pins to the version pex actually resolved on this run for the ranged requirement.
5. Commit the modification executed in 4. along with the introduction of the `python_binary` or else when it's requirements change.

For the pants binary itself (`src/python/pants/bin:pants_local_binary`), `enum34` is one example of this sort of hidden dep. It's required by both our `requests` and `pyopenssl` requirements (as well as our transitive `urllib3` requirement from `requests`).

Using 3 I find a line:
```
pex: Fetching file:///home/jsirois/.cache/pants/python_cache/requirements/CPython-2.7.15/enum34-1.1.6-py2-none-any.whl
```

So I could use this to implement step 4. above. and include the modified (or new) BUILDs / requirements.txt in a commit introducing the `python_binary`.

It seems to me this is all fairly unreasonable.

What would be more reasonable is for the Pants tasks that produce python binaries or their spiritual equivalent (`PythonRun` (only operates on `python_binary` targets), `PytestRun` (acts on a virtual test binary for a given set of targets) and `PythonRepl` (for a `python_binary` target) to emit some form of lock file that records exact version of all dependencies just resolved automatically. This file would then be checked in and Pants requirement resolution would learn to respect these files when present. One candidate would be a [`constraints`](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/user_guide/#constraints-files) file output in the same directory the `python_binary` target is declared in, perhaps named `(<target_name>.)constraints.txt` where `<target_name>.` could be omitted for the default target. This seems like it would work well for `python_binary` targets which have a natural home in the directory of the declaring BUILD file. For tests, which can glob many targets, this is problematic and I have no bright ideas given the current state of non-individual test-target requirement resolution. That though is fairly unambiguosly a bug (see #5406 and #4723) in v1 and will be fixed by design in v2, so maybe this idea flies.

To be clear, the proposal here results in the workflow:
0. No need to pin any direct requirements
1. Whenever a `python_binary` or `python_tests` target is "built", check in any new or modified `*constraints.txt` files.

The other detail to this proposal would be the embedding of a input-requirements hash in the generated `*constraints.txt` file such that is was not re-generated unless the input hash changed - thus providing stable resolves for any "binary" upon first check-in until such time as direct requirements were changed.

Conversion exists so you get a reliable type when accessing the attributes.

Think of conversion as a type cast - you can set an `Integer` field with `sys.max_int`, but not with `"a number"`. Validation is a further step that restricts the type, so for example you can restrict your `Integer` field to be no more that `1024`, so that `sys.max_int` fails validation.

Formally, conversion is a function that returns a type, while validation is a function that returns a boolean. Causing exception on conversion is a requirement, while raising exception on validation is a matter of convention.

We could perform late/lazy conversion, but seeing no further step can be done without it, makes sense to perform on the model init - and also because otherwise you would get an instance that causes exceptions on attribute access.
