@nfdisco ffmpeg has a bewildering variety of options, so without knowing exactly how you were using ffmpeg in comparison to livestreamer+mplayer, it's hard to say whether ffmpeg is inferior.  

For example, did you have ffmpeg download the file to disk?  If so, did you have it copy or reencode the streams?  Did you have it rewrite the timestamps to fix sync issues?  Did you enable fragmentation to allow seeking before the download finished?  Did you rewrite the file afterward without fragmentation to allow faster seeking?  Or did you have ffmpeg pass-through the data to a player program?  (In which case the player might be the one at fault; even VLC has issues with seeking incomplete files, especially older versions that are still in common use.)

My point is that, you might be right, that ffmpeg is simply inferior at the moment.  But it might be that ffmpeg already has the capability to perform just as well with the right settings; after all, it's practically a swiss-army-knife of video (de|en)coding, (de)muxing, and even (down|up)loading.

And then there is the issue of avconv vs. ffmpeg, and their differing abilities and command-line arguments...

### src/streamlink/plugins/yoursportsinhd.py
```py
import base64
import re

from streamlink.plugin import Plugin
from streamlink.plugin.api import http
from streamlink.stream import HLSStream
from streamlink.stream.hls_playlist import M3U8Parser

_url_re = re.compile(r"https?://(\w+\.)?yoursportsinhd.com")
_m3u8_re = re.compile(r"sources\:[\s\S]+src\:\s[\"\'](?:\s)?(?P<url>(?!http:\/\/nasatv)[^\"'\s]+)(?:\s)?[\"\']")


class YourSportsInHDM3U8Parser(M3U8Parser):
    redirect_test_1 = base64.b64decode("YnJvYWRiYW5kLmVzcG4uZ28uY29t=").decode("utf8")
    key_domain_1 = base64.b64decode("aHR0cDovL3lvdXJzcG9ydHNpbmhkLmNvbS9uY2FhL2dldF9rZXkv=").decode("utf8")

    redirect_test_2 = base64.b64decode("a2V5LXNlcnZpY2UudG90c3Vrby50dg==").decode("utf8")
    key_domain_2 = base64.b64decode("aHR0cDovL3lvdXJzcG9ydHNpbmhkLmNvbS92dWUvZ2V0X2tleS8=").decode("utf8")

    def uri(self, uri):
        if uri and "key" in uri:
            key_domain = ""
            if self.redirect_test_1 in uri:
                key_domain = self.key_domain_1
            elif self.redirect_test_2 in uri:
                key_domain = self.key_domain_2

            if key_domain:
                match_uri_key_re = re.compile(r"\?(?P<path_key>[\w=&/_\-.]+)")
                match = match_uri_key_re.search(uri)
                path_key = match.group("path_key")
                uri = "{0}{1}".format(key_domain, path_key)
        return super(YourSportsInHDM3U8Parser, self).uri(uri)


class YourSportsInHD(Plugin):
    @classmethod
    def can_handle_url(cls, url):
        return _url_re.match(url)

    def _get_streams(self):
        res = http.get(self.url)
        m3u8_url = _m3u8_re.search(res.text)
        hls_url = m3u8_url.group("url")

        if hls_url:
            for s in HLSStream.parse_variant_playlist(self.session,
                                                      hls_url,
                                                      parser=YourSportsInHDM3U8Parser).items():
                yield s

__plugin__ = YourSportsInHD

```

A second solution is to completely abandon class attributes. Class attributes work well when a single, unchanging definition is to be used globally by the Python interpreter. However, if this issue is to be solved successfully, then that will no longer be the case. If this issue is to be solved, then something like this should be possible:

```python
>>> hasattr(
...     Product(ServerConfig('http://old_sat.example.com', version='6.0')),
...     'sync_plan'
... )
False
>>> hasattr(
...     Product(ServerConfig('http://new_sat.example.com', version='6.1')),
...     'sync_plan'
... )
True
```

This could be made to work by moving field definitions in to the `__init__` method:

```python
class Product(Entity):
    def __init__(self, server_config, **kwargs):
        self._fields = {
            'description': entity_fields.StringField(),
            'label': entity_fields.StringField(),
            'name': entity_fields.StringField(required=True),
            'organization': entity_fields.OneToOneField(Organization, required=True),
            'sync_plan': entity_fields.OneToOneField(SyncPlan, null=True),
        }
        if server_config.version >= '6.1':
            self._fields['gpg_key'] = entity_fields.OneToOneField(GPGKey)
        super(Product, self).__init__(server_config, **kwargs)
```

There is at least one *very* nice side-effect of this approach. Currently, the entities contain lines such as the following:

    gpg_key = entity_fields.OneToOneField('GPGKey')

Using instance attributes allows us to name classes directly:

    'gpg_key': entity_fields.OneToOneField(GPGKey)

Naming classes directly would allow us to drop fragile stuff like `nailgun.entity_fields.ENTITIES_MODULE` and `nailgun.entity_fields._get_class`. (As I write this, I've just noticed a bug! The `ENTITIES_MODULE` variable is re-defined in module `nailgun.entities`. The bug is benign, as the variable is not changed. But still. This is subtle and could cause trouble.)

Having the IP addresses from which tests originate be widely variable and unpredictable until the moment of validation is actually a security feature and something we're planning to do more of, because it makes life much harder for attackers who aspire to defeat DV by compromising routers or DNS servers.

*In theory* we could probably tell the client a few seconds before the validation occurs, because the client is about to learn that fact anyway.  But that would require new protocol messages and additional protocol complexity, so I'm initially skeptical the operational payoff is worth that cost.

I suspect the problem is the following.

You are starting Python and running Ray using the binary `opt/anaconda3/bin/python`. However, Ray is starting a bunch of python workers, and it is just running the `python` command, so it is finding whichever version of python that `which python` returns. And that version of Python on your machine probably doesn't have numpy or redis installed.

If that's the case, you could work around this by doing `export PATH=opt/anaconda3/bin:$PATH` so that `which python` returns `opt/anaconda3/bin/python`.

But really, we should fix this by starting the workers using the same version of Python that you are using (and not just running the `python` executable). I'll look into this, I don't think it will be very difficult.

@bobloy We apologise for the confusion as Travis was installing a newer version of the stylechecking tool than the usual one. The issue has since been fixed, however we ask that you update your development requirements with `pipenv install --dev`.

Just as a side note, I would recommend running `pipenv install --dev` every time you pull/merge from V3/develop. Specifically, do it whenever you see that the `Pipfile.lock` file has been changed, for example here:

```bash
$ git pull
Updating 55afc7e..d3f406a
Fast-forward
 Pipfile.lock                           |  18 ++--
 ...
```

This usually means that a dependency has been pinned to a new version, and thus an update is necessary.

I found the cause. The validate function in _util.py causes the issue. I've gathered the related code here (run with python2 and python3 to see the differences in output):

```python
import re
request_line_re = re.compile("(?P<method>[-!#$%&'*+.^_`|~0-9a-zA-Z]+) (?P<target>[^ ]+) HTTP/(?P<http_version>[0-9]\\.[0-9])".encode("ascii"))

try:
    _fullmatch = type(re.compile('')).fullmatch
except AttributeError:
    def _fullmatch(regex, data):  # version specific: Python < 3.4
        match = regex.match(data)
        if match and match.end() != len(data):
            match = None
        return match

def validate(regex, data, msg="malformed data", *format_args):
    match = _fullmatch(regex, data)
    if not match:
        if format_args:
            msg = msg.format(*format_args)
        raise LocalProtocolError(msg)
    test = match.groupdict()
    return test

result = validate(request_line_re, bytearray(b'GET / HTTP/1.1'))
print(result)
```

```
{ blogEntries(first: 2) { edges { node { title author { name } } } } }

If the underlying data source were an RDBMS I would probably do a join like this:
```
SELECT blog_entry.blog_entry_id, blog_entry.title, author.author_id, author.name
FROM blog_entry JOIN author USING (author_id)
ORDER BY blog_entry.blog_entry_id
LIMIT 2
```
Then I could instantiate an `Author` object and attach it to each `BlogEntry` object in `resolve_blog_entries`.  Then I can check `info.root` in `resolve_blog_author` to see if it already has the Author instance available and if so, return it. ```
author = Author(**author_data)

blog_entry.author = author
```
But it would be a tad nicer IMO to be able to do the following, which isn’t allowed with the graphene types:

author = Author(**author_data)

I'm not following what you're doing in your code, but a couple things you might want to be aware of:

1. You will need to create a new shading element each time you want to apply it. If you `.append()` or otherwise dispose an element, `lxml` *removes* it from wherever it was, and moves it to the new location. Somewhat counterintuitive I know, but actually quite handy once you understand that's what `lxml` is going to do. So that could explain why some are ending up blank.

2. Any element parsed by `python-docx` will have the attribute `.xml`. This can be very handy for diagnostic purposes to print out work in progress, like:

   ```python
   for cell in row.cells:
       print(cell._tc.xml)
   ```

   This is often more convenient that inspecting the resulting XML with `opc-diag`, although I often use that latter method as well, perhaps something like:

   ```bash
   $ opc browse my-doc.docx document.xml | vim -
   ```

   Working with `python-docx` is all about getting the XML right, so this sort of inspection is the "final word" on how your code is working, so to speak.

   `opc-diag` is also handy for allowing manual edits to the XML so you can see what behaviors it produces in Word. This is what the `extract` and `repackage` subcommands are for.

By the way, your code has syntax errors. Also, I'm not at all sure what you're doing be accessing a ".shading_red" property on a CT_TcPr element (returned by `_tc.get_or_add_tcPr()`). There's no such property on that object. If you've assigned to it elsewhere in your code it will exist but not do anything to change the XML.

> 2018-07-20 22:19:12 [critical ] updateNonClosingBalanceProof failed [raiden.network.proxies.token_network]  ...
FATAL: Channel is not in a closed state  

On restart state changes which are not part of the snapshot are reapplied (currently all state changes), this includes the close and update transfer state changes. The problem is that some of the smart contract functions are identifying channels only by the pair `(addr1, addr2)`, e.g. [`getChannelInfo`](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-contracts/blob/585f8a014a869eb3e5e0983c54fb1ccaa4cc6144/raiden_contracts/contracts/TokenNetwork.sol#L1033-L1048), when the block state change which emits the update transfer is replayed the transaction for the *older* channel is re-sent, but the state check is done against the **new** channel.

IMO using the address pair is a terrible idea, we should just use the nonces to identify the channels.

BTrees.OOBTree.OOBucket([('a', 'a'), ('z', 'z')])

BTrees.OOBTree.OOBucket([('a', 'a'), ('z', 'z')])

The interface documentation uses the word "text" and "text" is the name of the package. Typically in zope packages that means unicode strings. 

However, [the test case](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.index/blob/master/src/zope/index/text/tests/test_htmlsplitter.py) simply passes native strings---bytes on Py2, text/unicode on Py3. The tests do [change the locale](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.index/blob/master/src/zope/index/text/tests/test_htmlsplitter.py#L51) and assert that things happen...BUT, [the docs for re.LOCALE](https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html#re.LOCALE) state that (emphasis mine):

> Make \w, \W, \b, \B, \s and \S dependent on the current locale. **The use of this flag is discouraged** as the locale mechanism is very unreliable, and it only handles one “culture” at a time anyway; **you should use Unicode matching instead, which is the default in Python 3 for Unicode (str) patterns**. This flag can be used only with bytes patterns.

So I suspect that on Python 3, we don't want the `(?L)` flag at all.

> 1) Why do we dump the JSON into the filesystem? The XML was being dumped before so that the bibupload task could upload the record, not sure if it is needed now.

Because we should dump everything what we get from the user without modifying it. That's for the case that someone wants to re-run the deposit on a new version, a different platform or after something went terribly wrong. Bibupload is dead. It is a bad thing which was designed before we were using a proper ACID database.

> 2) Why seal the SIP at the beginning and have to change all the calls to d.get_latest_sip(sealed=True) instead of d.get_latest_sip(sealed=False) ? That breaks all our tasks that were expecting seal==False

It is not a SIP anyway and we never had a proper one. It is a best effort to show that we already dumped the user input and that everything you do now to the SIP won't be reproduced by other software. We now have
 - `unsealed` = as long as we are collecting data from the forms, uploads or (later, maybe) harvesters. You are free to ask for additional user input or clarifications. You are not allowed to add interpretations or extracted data to the record or to normalize the input.
 - `sealed` = everything is collected. We start our own internal processing. Whatever you do now won't be transmitted to SIP-backup systems (or other libraries). That gives you freedom but also the restriction to never ask for additional user input. And by never we mean never. The deposit is completed and you are one your own at this stage.

What might be important (and is written down in the commit message): We need to rework the SIP/DIP/... thing in the future to make it (finally, it never was) standard compliant. If you (or everyone else) wants to volunteer, feel free to sit down with some library people, discuss the best solution, implement it, submit a PR, wait for the OK from the Invenio team and the librarians and then finally make Invenio a proper library system.

Last little note: as far as we know we do NOT need to use MarcXML to conform with the standard. We were even wrong when storing the transformed user input as an SIP (without any other packages), because the form data (=JSON) to marc conversion is a heavy modification of the data and might destroy reproducibility.

The problem with `cascade` (as quoted in https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/issues/2419#issuecomment-59476170) is that the backend is unaware of this SQLAlchemy construct. One has to inform the model of this explicitly. Here is an example:

```python
class Workflow(db.Model):  # Parent
    __tablename__ = "bwlWORKFLOW"
    uuid = db.Column(...)
    child_logs = db.relationship("DbWorkflowEngineLog",
                                  backref='bwlWORKFLOW', cascade="all, delete-orphan")

class DbWorkflowEngineLog(db.Model):  # Children
    __tablename__ = "bwlWORKFLOWLOGGING"
    id_workflow = db.Column(db.String(36),
                            db.ForeignKey("bwlWORKFLOW.uuid", ondelete='CASCADE'),  # <---
                             nullable=True)
```

In fact, `ondelete='CASCADE'` is neccessary with InnoDB as it enforces the constraints.

This a common problem in tokenization. Unfortunately just adding the period raises problems with abbreviations. The nltk punkt package adresses this issue:

http://www.nltk.org/api/nltk.tokenize.html

There are also re-implementations of this algorithm in other languages:

https://github.com/lfcipriani/punkt-segmenter
https://github.com/ferristseng/rust-punkt

A simple workaround would be to preprocess the text and detect abbreviations and replace them by the full word or add them to the vocabulary.
Fanciest solution would be to integrate the punkt tokenizer directly (by re-implementing or by the usage of one of the mentioned  libs).

Yes, afp-core uses the user's groups to check what he may access. So no matter what you send in the URL, there's a fixed set of account+role combinations that you may access. The request only serves to select one of them.

mriehl's point is valid, though. Via
```
afp-cli accountA ../accountB/myrole
```
I will actually log into accountB, not accountA (assuming, of course, I have access to accountB). Not sure if that is an issue, though, it's just how URLs work and there's no privilege esacaltion.

The `#` does not allow you to select a different account. We could URL-encode the parameters to allow role names that contain characters like %#/ and potentially any UTF-8 character.

So, `AuthorizedHttp.request` *shouldn't* wrap exceptions at all. It should behave *exactly* like the thing it's wrapping. Conversely, `Request` *should* wrap exceptions. The difference here is that `AuthorizedHttp` is supposed to be a user-facing replacement for `httplib2.Http` and should more or less stick to the substitution principle, whereas `Request` is an internal facing construct that allows this library to be framework agnostic.
